nativeRange.habitat: Shaded reef walls, caves, crevices, and undersides of overhangs; frequently found on artificial structures such as shipwrecks; thrives in areas of reduced light and strong current

diet.notes: Strictly heterotrophic — contains no zooxanthellae and derives no energy from light. Requires daily target-feeding of meaty zooplankton directly to each polyp: frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, copepods, rotifers, finely chopped clam, scallop, or shrimp are all accepted. Turkey baster or pipette target-feeding is strongly recommended. Live copepods (e.g., tigger pods) are particularly effective. Amino acid dosing can supplement but does not replace particulate feeding.

goodWith: other non-photosynthetic corals (Chili Coral, Sea Fans, Tree Coral)
avoidWith: aggressive corals with sweeper tentacles (Galaxea, Hammer, Torch)
fast-growing photosynthetic corals that may overgrow
algae-prone areas where overgrowth can smother the base
food-stealing fish (wrasses, triggers, large angelfish) in feeding proximity

summary: Tubastraea sun corals are iconic azooxanthellate NPS corals producing vivid orange or yellow polyps; no light is required but daily target-feeding to every polyp is mandatory for survival. Advanced care due to feeding demands.
careNotes:
Tubastraea is a genus of non-photosynthetic (NPS) LPS corals — they contain no zooxanthellae and cannot derive energy from light. The most common aquarium species are T. faulkneri (orange/yellow polyps) and T. coccinea (smaller orange polyps). T. micranthus produces striking black/brown colony forms.

FEEDING — THE PRIMARY CHALLENGE: Daily target-feeding to every individual polyp is the single most critical husbandry requirement. Most aquarium losses are attributed to underfeeding or irregular feeding. Polyps naturally extend tentacles at night, but experienced keepers condition them to open at any time by initiating consistent feeding routines. Target-feed with a turkey baster or pipette, directing food (frozen mysis, brine shrimp, copepods, chopped seafood) at each polyp opening. A single colony with many polyps may require 10–20 minutes of feeding per session. TidalGardens describes successful keepers feeding 'between a dozen to two dozen small feedings' per day via automated dosing equipment, though 1–2 substantial daily feedings with a baster is a viable approach for most hobbyists. Never allow more than 1–2 days between feedings.

LIGHTING: Requires no light for energy — placement in the darkest areas of the tank is fine and often preferred. Keep PAR under 75 to avoid tissue stress. Can be placed in overhangs, cave ceilings, or the shaded underside of rockwork. The colony's vibrant color is independent of light.

FLOW: Moderate to high water movement overall benefits the colony by delivering planktonic food, but briefly reducing flow around the colony during feeding sessions improves polyp capture rates. Placement a few inches downstream from a powerhead can create beneficial swirling current.

PLACEMENT: Can be placed anywhere in the tank, including inverted under overhangs. Avoid sandy substrates beneath the colony, as contact with shifting sand can damage tissue. Elevate on stable rockwork.

NUTRIENT LOAD: Heavy feeding means heavy nutrient input. An oversized protein skimmer and aggressive water changes are generally advised to manage nitrate and phosphate accumulation from regular feeding.

FRAGGING: Relatively easy — colonies can be fragged with bone cutters; individual polyps with attached skeleton will regrow.

INVASIVE WARNING: Tubastraea coccinea is established as an invasive species on Caribbean and Atlantic reefs, spreading from its original Indo-Pacific range. It was documented in the Caribbean as early as 1943, likely via ship ballast water, and has spread north to the Florida Keys. Never release aquarium specimens, tank water, or associated materials into Atlantic, Gulf of Mexico, or Caribbean waters. Proper disposal (desiccation, bleach, or sealed landfill) is required.

Variant note: T. faulkneri and T. coccinea are most common in the trade. T. micranthus (black sun coral) has similar husbandry needs.
breedingNotes: Tubastraea reproduce via broadcast spawning of planulae larvae in the wild. In captivity, fragmentation is the most accessible propagation method — colonies can be divided with bone cutters and individual polyp clusters will encrust and grow. Sexual reproduction is not reliably achieved in aquaria. The species' capacity for rapid asexual reproduction via larval settlement partly explains its success as an invasive species on Atlantic reefs.

primary.name: LiveAquaria — Orange Tube Coral
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters (temperature 72–78°F, salinity 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, kH 8–12), lighting level (low), flow (medium to strong), placement (bottom), care level (moderate), feeding method (target-feed each polyp). Temperature converted to Celsius: 72°F = 22.2°C, 78°F = 25.6°C.

additional.name: AquariumSource — Sun Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (temperature 77–80°F / 25–26.7°C, salinity 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, kH 8–12, calcium ~425 ppm, magnesium 1200–1350 ppm), lighting max 75 PAR, flow moderate 20–40x turnover, minimum tank 10 gal (38 L), colony size ~6 in diameter (15 cm), feeding 3–5x/week minimum. Temperature upper end 26.7°C averaged with LiveAquaria lower end to arrive at 24–26°C range. Care level listed as 'moderate'; bumped to 'advanced' per feeding-burden and invasive-awareness factors.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens — Tubastraea Coral Care
additional.notes: Expert-level specialist source. Confirmed PAR under 75, calcium ~400 ppm, magnesium ~1350 ppm, kH 8–11 (wild ocean ~8–9). Detailed feeding guidance: up to dozens of small feedings per day for optimal growth; colony can be conditioned to open during daytime with consistent feeding. Care level described as 'very high maintenance — not recommended unless reef keeper is really committed.' Confirmed Indo-Pacific native range (Fiji, Tonga, Solomon Islands, Great Barrier Reef).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Tubastraea
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family: Dendrophylliidae, Order: Scleractinia), native range context, invasive status of T. coccinea (Caribbean since 1943, Florida Keys by 2004), and species identification (T. coccinea, T. faulkneri, T. micranthus). Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn — Keeping and Feeding the Sun Corals
additional.notes: Confirmed NPS classification, placement under overhangs/cave ceilings, strong flow for food delivery, importance of live copepods (tigger pods) as prey. Emphasized 'The key to success is proper nutrition.' Used as supporting source for care notes prose.
